metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `timeout=<short>` with a stated default value (e.g. 'match the `timeout` used in `reconverge`'s `write_lock` call, or default to 30 s') so the executor has an actionable value without a design detour.
findings.finding: The `timeout=<short>` value in the What/Files section is a literal placeholder, deferring the actual lock-timeout value choice to the executor rather than stating a default.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the placeholder with: `timeout=<reconverge_timeout>` and add a note: 'use the same timeout value already passed to `write_lock` in `sync.py::reconverge`; if none exists, default to 30 s'. This makes it a defaultable decision with a clear rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how the new regression test achieves true concurrency (e.g., hold the write lock in a background thread while calling `push_tickets_branch` in the foreground, or use `monkeypatch` to inject a delay inside the merge critical section), so the test actually exercises the race window rather than sequential calls.
findings.finding: The plan's AC specifies a regression test 'using the `two_clones` fixture in `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py`' but does not name the specific test function or describe what 'a concurrent locked write during a push-retry merge' means at the implementation level (e.g., whether it uses threading, a subprocess, monkeypatching, or a timing hook). The `two_clones` fixture provisions two full CLI-driven clones and a bare remote; it is unclear whether a true concurrent write can be expressed within it without a threading or subprocess harness that the plan does not describe. Without this specificity the implementer may produce a test that does not actually exercise the concurrency window (e.g. sequential calls that never overlap).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 1 and AC line 3; What/files section (test specification)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `logger.warning(...)` (or equivalent metric increment) in the `LockTimeout` skip branch of `push_tickets_branch`, and assert a WARNING log record for that path in the concurrency regression test (or a dedicated unit test).
findings.finding: The LockTimeout skip path is a new silent failure mode with no specified logging. When lock contention causes the merge to be skipped in REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=async mode, no log entry, metric, or alert is mandated, so operators cannot observe or diagnose accumulating push-skip events.
findings.location: What / files section and AC line 1 ('On `_lock.LockTimeout`, skip the merge and leave the push pending')
findings.suggested_fix: In the `except _lock.LockTimeout` handler, emit: `logger.warning('push_tickets_branch: write-lock timeout acquiring merge lock; push left pending (best-effort)', extra={'base_path': str(base_path)})`. Add `assert any('push left pending' in r.message for r in caplog.records)` to the regression test that exercises the timeout path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (or add a subtitle) to reflect both changes, e.g. 'Take the write lock around the push-retry reconcile merge and log async-spawn OSError', so the headline faithfully covers all AC items.
findings.finding: The plan's title scopes the work to 'take the write lock around the push-retry reconcile merge', but the body and Acceptance Criteria also include an independent OSError-logging fix for the async detached-spawn path. This second item appears nowhere in the title, making the headline an incomplete description of what will actually be built.
findings.location: Title vs. 'What / files' section and AC line 2 ('The async-push detached-spawn failure path logs at WARNING')
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the title to: 'Harden push-retry: write-lock the reconcile merge and log async-spawn failures' — or add a one-line 'Also:' clause beneath the main title.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `push_after_commit` docstring in `push.py` to clarify that calling `push_tickets_branch` while holding the write lock will deadlock (not merely violate a design preference), since after this change `push_tickets_branch` acquires the write lock internally.
findings.finding: After the plan is implemented, `push_tickets_branch` internally acquires the write lock, making any call to it while the write lock is already held a deadlock. The `push_after_commit` docstring in push.py (line 243–245) currently says 'the push runs its own fetch/merge and must not nest inside the write lock' as a soft design rationale; post-fix this becomes a hard correctness constraint (deadlock). The plan names push.py as an edit target but does not include updating this docstring to reflect the stronger invariant. This leaves callers with a misleadingly soft-sounding warning about what is now a deadlock condition.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the docstring phrase from 'the push runs its own fetch/merge and must not nest inside the write lock' to 'the push acquires the write lock internally — calling it while the write lock is held will deadlock; always call AFTER the lock is released'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving assertion for 'no event is lost' in AC3 — e.g. 'assert that both the concurrent write's event and any pre-existing push-pending events appear in the store after the merge (verified by asserting `len(store.read_events()) == expected_count` in the regression test)'.
findings.finding: AC3's 'no event is lost' sub-claim lacks a concrete proving command or observable assertion, making it outcome-vague and unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'the write succeeds (no spurious StoreError) and no event is lost'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC3 to: '...no event is lost — verified by asserting that `store.read_events()` contains all N expected events (count-checked with `assert len(...) == N`) after the concurrent push-retry merge and write complete.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope's 'one concurrency regression test' with AC1 and AC3 requiring two distinct scenarios: either combine them into a single parameterised test explicitly covering both the lock-timeout-skip path AND the concurrent-write-success path, or update the Scope to say 'two concurrency regression tests'.
findings.finding: Scope says 'one concurrency regression test' but AC1 and AC3 together require two distinct concurrency test scenarios: (1) lock-timeout → merge skipped (AC1), and (2) concurrent write during push-retry merge → write succeeds, no event lost (AC3). An implementer following the Scope literally would write one test and miss either the timeout path or the concurrent-write-success path.
findings.location: Scope section ('one concurrency regression test') vs. AC1 and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope to 'two concurrency regression tests (lock-timeout skip + concurrent write success)' and ensure AC1 and AC3 each call out their own named test function, e.g. `test_push_retry_lock_timeout_skips_merge` and `test_concurrent_write_during_push_retry_succeeds`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verifiable note to AC1 (or a new AC) requiring that the `write_lock` call in `push_tickets_branch` mirrors the sync.py call signature including `dual_window=True`, so a code-review check or test can confirm the parameter is present.
findings.finding: The What/files section specifies `dual_window=True` as a required argument to `_lock.write_lock(...)`, but no AC verifies this parameter is passed. An implementer could satisfy all ACs while omitting `dual_window=True`, potentially diverging from the pattern established in sync.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: '…and the `write_lock` call matches the `sync.py::_do_reconverge` call signature including `dual_window=True`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement step (e.g. a fixture-driven timing probe or a note in the regression test) that derives or validates the `timeout=<short>` value against realistic concurrent-write load, rather than asserting it as an unspecified placeholder.
findings.finding: The `timeout=<short>` for the write_lock call in push.py is asserted as 'short' but no measurement plan, spike, or derivation from fixture data is provided to validate what value avoids both spurious merge-skips (too short) and excessive async-push blocking (too long).
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'timeout should be derived from the existing sync.py write_lock call (mirror its value) OR measured via the two_clones fixture under concurrent write load; document the chosen value and its rationale inline.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the write_lock timeout into a named module-level constant (or reference the same constant used by sync.py) rather than an inline magic number, so tuning and mirroring are explicit.
findings.finding: The `timeout=<short>` threshold will be hardcoded in push.py implementation with no plan to make it configurable or derive it from the existing sync.py constant, making future tuning require a code change.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `_PUSH_LOCK_TIMEOUT_S = <value>` at module level in push.py (or import from a shared _lock_config module if one exists), mirroring however sync.py exposes its timeout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specification (in 'What / files' or AC) for the code action when the inner `check_no_rebase_in_progress` re-check fails: either skip-and-log-WARNING (consistent with LockTimeout best-effort) or raise a named exception, and add an AC or test assertion covering that branch.
findings.finding: The plan mandates re-checking `check_no_rebase_in_progress` inside the lock but does not specify the code action when the inner check finds a rebase IS in progress — leaving the implementer to guess between raise/skip/log, risking either a spurious exception escaping the lock context or a silent no-op.
findings.location: What / files section: 'Re-check `_lock.check_no_rebase_in_progress(base_path)` INSIDE the locked critical section'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'If the inner rebase check fires, log at WARNING and skip the merge (leave push pending), matching the LockTimeout behaviour — do not raise from inside the lock.' Add a corresponding test that patches `check_no_rebase_in_progress` to raise after lock acquisition and asserts the WARNING log and no exception escapes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the `timeout` for the new `write_lock` call to an explicit short constant (e.g., `_PUSH_MERGE_LOCK_TIMEOUT = 5` or similar) documented as 'push merge is best-effort; a held lock means skip', mirroring `_SYNC_LOCK_TIMEOUT` in sync.py — do not leave it as `<short>` for the implementer to guess.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'timeout=<short>' for the new write_lock call but leaves the actual value unspecified. The timeout is load-bearing for the invariant that the async background push child must never block foreground writers: too large a value (e.g., the module default 30s×2=60s) means the background child holds the lock across git network I/O durations, stalling foreground writes for potentially minutes. sync.py uses _SYNC_LOCK_TIMEOUT=15s for exactly this reason; push.py's merge lock needs an analogous explicit cap.
findings.location: What/files section: 'wrap the merge/stash mutation in `with _lock.write_lock(base_path, timeout=<short>, attempts=1, dual_window=True):`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_PUSH_MERGE_LOCK_TIMEOUT = 5` (or ≤ the shortest foreground write budget) at the top of push.py and use it as `timeout=_PUSH_MERGE_LOCK_TIMEOUT` in the write_lock call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that the `write_lock` is acquired and released PER NON-FF RETRY ITERATION — covering only `check_no_rebase_in_progress` + `git merge` + stash dance — and that `git push` itself runs OUTSIDE the lock (released before the next `continue` sends the loop back to the push call).
findings.finding: The plan says 'wrap the merge/stash mutation in `with _lock.write_lock(...)`' but does not specify whether this lock acquisition lives INSIDE the per-attempt retry loop or OUTSIDE it (wrapping all 3 attempts). Wrapping the outer loop would hold the lock across multiple `git push` network calls (each bounded to _GIT_TIMEOUT=30s), creating a worst-case lock hold of 3×30s=90s in the async child — far exceeding any sane foreground write budget. The correct placement is inside the loop (lock per non-FF merge attempt only), but the plan's prose is ambiguous enough that an implementer could reasonably wrap the loop.
findings.location: What/files section: 'wrap the merge/stash mutation in `with _lock.write_lock(base_path, timeout=<short>, attempts=1, dual_window=True):`'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
